title: Stage Contracts
use_for: defining explicit stage inputs and outputs
naming product contracts between pipeline stages
separating owned products from incidental internal state
do_not_use_for: generic OOP interfaces detached from dataflow products
rules: define stage contracts around owned products, not around arbitrary method surfaces
name inputs and outputs explicitly at each stage boundary
keep incidental internal state out of cross-stage contracts
stabilize contracts before complicating dispatch or invalidation
prefer narrow product contracts over broad mutable pass-through state
procedure: List each stage's consumed inputs and produced outputs.
Separate owned products from internal working state.
Define stable contracts for cross-stage products.
Verify downstream stages depend on named products rather than hidden side effects.
Review whether contract width matches recompute and invalidation needs.

primary_when: the request is specifically about stage input/output shape, product contracts, or dependency surfaces between stages
the request is about making cross-stage coupling explicit and stable
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about dispatch policy, batching policy, or local product invalidation with no contract ambiguity

solves: how stages should describe what they consume and produce
how to keep downstream dependencies pointed at named products instead of hidden side effects
how to reduce coupling between stages without hiding the dataflow
does_not_solve: whether the overall pipeline should exist
feature-domain semantics outside stage product flow

core_principles: A stage contract should describe owned product flow, not generic methods.
Downstream stages should depend on named products, not ambient mutation.
Narrow contracts improve recompute clarity and invalidation review.
constraints: Stage contracts must remain explicit enough to review without running the system.
Internal working state should not leak into cross-stage dependency surfaces.
Contract changes should be visible enough to reason about structural rerun or invalidation impact.
decision_rules: Use stage-contracts as the owner when the main issue is ambiguity in what stages consume, produce, or depend on.
If stage boundaries are still unclear, attach stage-boundaries before finalizing contracts.
If contracts change often because products are too broad, narrow the product surface before adding more stages.
If contract changes imply topology changes, attach structural-rerun-policy to keep fallback explicit.

preferred_patterns: named input and output products per stage
internal scratch state kept local to the stage
dependency surfaces aligned with recompute boundaries
avoid_patterns: broad mutable pass-through objects spanning many stages
stage interfaces that hide what data actually moves
downstream dependence on incidental internal state
contract growth that mirrors implementation detail rather than product flow

recipes.name: Define product contracts between stages
recipes.when: Use when stages exist but downstream coupling is still vague or too broad.
steps: Separate it from internal stage scratch state.
Make downstream stages depend only on the named product.
Review whether contract width matches the needed recompute scope.

pitfalls: Treating stage contracts as generic interfaces instead of product definitions.
Letting internal working state leak into downstream dependencies.
Passing one broad mutable object through many stages.
review_checklist: Does each stage expose named inputs and outputs?
Are downstream dependencies tied to products rather than side effects?
Is internal scratch state kept out of the stage contract?
Do contracts stay narrow enough for recompute review?

references.title: Local Reference: Stage Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this first for stage input/output design, product naming, and stable cross-stage dependency surfaces.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for explicit stage contracts.

references.title: Local Reference: Stage Boundaries
references.interpretation: Use this to keep stage contracts aligned with real boundary ownership and recompute economics.
references.why: Use to connect contract design with existing stage-boundary guidance.
